h1Title: Recurrent Nosebleeds (Epistaxis): Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
heroOverview: Recurrent epistaxis — nosebleeds occurring two or more times per month — is one of the most common ENT presentations, affecting approximately 60% of the general population at some point in their lives, with 6% seeking medical care. Nosebleeds originate from the anterior nasal septum in 90-95% of cases at Kiesselbach's plexus, a convergence of four arterial branches. Most anterior bleeds are benign and self-limiting, but posterior bleeds from the sphenopalatine artery territory are less common (5-10%), more severe, and carry higher risks of airway compromise and significant blood loss. Recurrence signals an underlying cause in 20-30% of adults, including hypertension, anticoagulation, coagulation disorders, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, or nasal neoplasia. Properly directed treatment resolves recurrent bleeding in over 90% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: 60% of people experience at least one nosebleed in their lifetime; approximately 6% seek medical care (NICE 2023); emergency department visits for epistaxis in the US exceed 450,000 per year
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution: peak in children under 10 and adults over 50. HHT affects 1 in 5,000-8,000 people and accounts for 5% of recurrent epistaxis cases.
keyStats.avgAge: Childhood epistaxis peaks ages 2-10 from digital trauma and dry air; adult epistaxis peaks after 50 related to hypertension, anticoagulants, and mucosal atrophy
keyStats.globalCases: ~1 in 7 Americans will seek medical care for a nosebleed in their lifetime; posterior epistaxis requiring hospital admission accounts for approximately 5-10% of presenting cases

definition: Recurrent epistaxis (ICD-10: R04.0, epistaxis-recurrent) refers to nasal hemorrhage occurring repeatedly — typically defined as two or more episodes per month — rather than a single isolated event. Epistaxis is classified anatomically as anterior or posterior. Anterior epistaxis (90-95%) originates from Kiesselbach's plexus on the antero-inferior nasal septum at Little's area, where the anterior ethmoid, sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries converge; these vessels are superficial and susceptible to drying, trauma, and inflammation. Posterior epistaxis (5-10%) arises from the sphenopalatine artery branches in the posterior nasal cavity, is harder to control, and more often requires specialist intervention. Recurrent epistaxis differs from isolated epistaxis in that it implies either a local anatomical abnormality (prominent vessels, telangiectasia, septal perforation, polyp, neoplasm) or a systemic predisposing factor (coagulopathy, anticoagulant therapy, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, uncontrolled hypertension). The condition is primarily managed by otolaryngologists with possible involvement of hematology, internal medicine, and interventional radiology for complex or refractory cases.

typesClassification.description: 90-95% of all nosebleeds; blood appears at the front of one nostril; typically mild to moderate volume; most amenable to first-aid pinching and topical treatment.
5-10% of cases; blood runs down the throat and may not be visible at the nostril; often bilateral; associated with greater blood loss; requires nasal packing or endoscopic surgery.

Autosomal dominant vascular disorder (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome) causing recurrent mucosal telangiectasias; affects 1 in 5,000-8,000 people; 95% have recurrent nosebleeds by age 45; requires specialized management distinct from idiopathic recurrent epistaxis.

Most childhood nosebleeds are idiopathic, arising from anterior septum dryness and digital trauma. Von Willebrand disease is found in 5-10% of children investigated for frequent nosebleeds. The majority resolve spontaneously with puberty as nasal mucosa matures.

primarySymptoms: Visible blood from one or both nostrils, often appearing spontaneously without known trauma, characteristically in the morning or during dry indoor heating season.
Blood dripping down the back of the throat (posterior bleed), which may be swallowed and present as nausea, vomiting of blood, or dark stools without visible anterior hemorrhage.
Two or more bleeding episodes per month in adults or children, defining the recurrent pattern that warrants investigation beyond simple first aid.
Prolonged bleeding lasting more than 20 minutes despite sustained nasal compression, suggesting a posterior source, coagulopathy, or significant underlying vascular disease.
Bilateral or alternating nosebleeds are more characteristic of posterior sphenopalatine artery bleeding or HHT rather than the typically unilateral anterior septum origin.
Visible red spots on the nasal mucosa, lips, tongue, or fingertips (telangiectasias) accompanying frequent nosebleeds — hallmark features of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia.
Nasal crusting, dryness, or septal discomfort preceding or following bleeds, indicating mucosal fragility from drying, inflammation, or intranasal steroid use.
Pallor, lightheadedness, or syncope from cumulative blood loss in patients with very frequent or particularly heavy recurrent nosebleeds requiring hemoglobin assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: Two or more nosebleeds per month in an adult over 50 with hypertension or on anticoagulants — most likely an identifiable and modifiable precipitant requiring medication review.
Family history of recurrent nosebleeds combined with multiple visible telangiectasias on lips, tongue, or fingertips — suggests HHT and warrants genetic testing.
Unilateral nasal obstruction with recurrent unilateral bleeding in an adult over 40 — must exclude nasopharyngeal or sinonasal neoplasm until proven otherwise.
Iron-deficiency anemia on blood count in a patient with frequent nosebleeds — confirms cumulative blood loss exceeding what is clinically apparent.
emergencySigns: Bleeding lasting more than 30 minutes despite sustained firm nasal compression and first-aid measures — requires emergency ENT assessment for nasal packing or cautery.

Hemodynamic instability (systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg, heart rate above 110 bpm, altered consciousness) from epistaxis — requires emergency resuscitation, blood transfusion assessment, and urgent hemostasis.
Posterior bleed with blood running entirely down the throat, causing choking, airway distress, or aspiration — emergency airway management and posterior nasal packing required.

Epistaxis in a patient on anticoagulation with supratherapeutic INR or in a patient with known thrombocytopenia or hemophilia — requires hematology and ENT joint assessment and likely reversal of anticoagulation.

causes.cause: Local mucosal dryness and digital trauma
causes.description: The most common cause, particularly in children. Low humidity from central heating or air conditioning dries nasal mucosa; nose-picking (rhinotilleximania) disrupts the delicate vessels at Kiesselbach's plexus. This benign combination accounts for the majority of recurrent childhood epistaxis and most summer and winter seasonal peaks.

Systolic blood pressure above 160 mmHg is present in approximately 60-70% of adults presenting to emergency departments with epistaxis. Whether hypertension is a direct cause or merely associated through shared cardiovascular risk factors remains debated; however, uncontrolled hypertension prolongs bleeding once it starts and impairs the normal vasoconstriction response. Blood pressure control is an essential part of epistaxis management in affected adults.

Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran), aspirin, clopidogrel, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs impair clotting or platelet function. Patients on anticoagulants have a 3-5× higher epistaxis rate than the general population and are more likely to require nasal packing or endoscopic cautery. Supratherapeutic INR should be identified and corrected urgently.

Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder (affecting 1% of the population) and presents in 5-10% of children with recurrent nosebleeds investigated by hematology. Hemophilia A and B, and rare platelet function disorders, also present with mucosal bleeding including epistaxis. A structured bleeding history (ISTH-BAT score) identifies patients warranting hematological workup.

An autosomal dominant disorder of TGF-β signaling (ENG, ACVRL1, and SMAD4 gene mutations) causing multiple mucocutaneous telangiectasias. Recurrent nosebleeds are the presenting symptom in over 95% of HHT patients by age 45, averaging 18 episodes per month in severe cases. HHT also causes arteriovenous malformations in the lungs, liver, and brain, making diagnosis important beyond epistaxis management.

causes.cause: Intranasal steroid spray or nasal decongestant overuse
causes.description: Topical corticosteroid sprays directed incorrectly at the nasal septum (rather than the lateral wall) cause septal mucosal atrophy and telangiectasia formation, predisposing to recurrent anterior bleeding. Oxymetazoline (Afrin) decongestant rebound congestion and vasoconstriction cycles stress nasal mucosal vasculature. Proper spray technique instruction prevents most steroid-related epistaxis.

riskFactors.description: Systolic blood pressure above 160 mmHg is strongly associated with epistaxis severity and recurrence. Blood pressure control reduces epistaxis frequency; ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred antihypertensives in patients with recurrent epistaxis.

Warfarin with INR above 3.0, DOACs, aspirin, and clopidogrel all prolong bleeding time. Patients on combination antithrombotic therapy have the highest epistaxis risk. Medication review and dose optimization reduce recurrence without increasing thromboembolic risk in most patients.

riskFactors.factor: Low ambient humidity (dry climate or heating season)
riskFactors.description: Indoor humidity below 30% dries nasal mucosa, causes crusting, and promotes vessel fragility. Regular nasal saline irrigation and nasal lubricants reduce seasonally-driven recurrent epistaxis by restoring mucosal moisture.

Autosomal dominant trait; first-degree relative with HHT confers 50% inheritance risk. Curacao criteria diagnose HHT when epistaxis, telangiectasias, family history, and visceral AVM are present. Diagnosis in a proband triggers screening of first-degree relatives.

riskFactors.factor: Nasal inflammatory conditions (allergic rhinitis, chronic sinusitis)
riskFactors.description: Persistent nasal inflammation from allergy, chronic rhinosinusitis, or infection thins the nasal mucosa and promotes vessel engorgement. Treatment of the underlying inflammatory condition reduces epistaxis recurrence by 30-50%.

Septal perforation after septoplasty, cocaine abuse, or nasal trauma creates a turbulent airflow zone at the perforation edge, causing mucosal dryness, crusting, and recurrent bleeding at the perforation rim.

Nasal mucosal atrophy, vessel wall stiffening, and increasing use of anticoagulants and antiplatelets converge in older adults to dramatically increase epistaxis severity and recurrence. Posterior bleeds are proportionally more common in those over 60.

affectedDemographics: Children ages 2-10: anterior septum digital trauma and dry air are the primary causes; most cases are benign and resolve with puberty.
Adults over 50: most common presentation to emergency departments and ENT outpatients; hypertension, anticoagulants, and mucosal atrophy are the predominant drivers.
Patients with HHT (hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia): any age from childhood; 95% develop epistaxis by age 45; very high frequency — median 18 episodes per month in moderate-severe HHT.
People on anticoagulation therapy: 3-5× higher epistaxis incidence than the general population regardless of INR; risk increases with combination antithrombotic regimens.
diagnosisOverview: History-taking focuses on frequency, duration, laterality, whether blood comes forward from the nostril or backward into the throat, blood loss volume (soaking count), and triggers. Medications — especially anticoagulants, NSAIDs, and nasal sprays — must be reviewed. A structured bleeding history using the International Society on Thrombosis and Haemostasis Bleeding Assessment Tool (ISTH-BAT) identifies coagulopathy risk. Anterior rhinoscopy (nasal speculum plus headlight) identifies prominent vessels, telengiecasias, Kiesselbach's plexus vessels, septal perforations, polyps, or mass lesions. Rigid nasal endoscopy under local anesthesia provides definitive visualization of the posterior nasal cavity and sphenopalatine artery territory when anterior rhinoscopy is unremarkable. Blood tests in adults with recurrent significant epistaxis should include full blood count, coagulation screen (PT, APTT), and INR if on warfarin. Von Willebrand factor antigen, ristocetin cofactor, and factor VIII are assayed when VWD is suspected (family history, ISTH-BAT score ≥4 in adults). CT of the sinuses and nasopharynx is indicated when unilateral bleeding or nasal obstruction suggests an underlying mass. Diagnosis of HHT requires meeting at least three Curacao criteria: spontaneous recurrent epistaxis, mucocutaneous telangiectasias, visceral AVM, and affected first-degree relative.

diagnosticTests.test: Anterior rhinoscopy with headlight and nasal speculum
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Kiesselbach's plexus vessels, telengiecasias, septal deviation, mucosal disease, or mass lesion responsible for anterior epistaxis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician inserts a nasal speculum and examines the nasal cavity under bright illumination. Takes 2-3 minutes, mildly uncomfortable but not painful. Usually performed in primary care or the ENT outpatient clinic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes the posterior nasal cavity, sphenopalatine artery area, nasopharynx, and any mass lesion not accessible to anterior rhinoscopy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A rigid 0° or 30° 4 mm scope is passed along the nasal floor after local anesthetic and decongestant spray. Takes 5-10 minutes; mildly uncomfortable. Images are recorded for documentation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects thrombocytopenia, platelet dysfunction, anemia from blood loss, coagulopathy, and warfarin supratherapeutic INR as contributing causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results within 1-3 hours in an emergency setting. Warfarin INR should be checked for all patients on warfarin presenting with epistaxis.

diagnosticTests.test: Von Willebrand factor panel (VWF antigen, ristocetin cofactor, factor VIII)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses von Willebrand disease, the most common inherited bleeding disorder, which presents with mucosal bleeding including recurrent nosebleeds
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw with results in 3-5 days from a hematology laboratory. Requires fasting and no prior aspirin for 7 days for accurate results; check with the lab for specific requirements.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for sinonasal neoplasm, juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (young males), vascular malformations, or bony destruction not visible on endoscopy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-minute CT scan without contrast for primary evaluation. Contrast is added if a vascular or malignant lesion is suspected. No preparation required for unenhanced CT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Structured clinical bleeding history score that identifies patients requiring hematological investigation; scores ≥4 in adults or ≥3 in children indicate significant bleeding phenotype
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A questionnaire administered by the clinician, scoring 14 types of bleeding symptoms. Takes 5-10 minutes. A high score prompts VWD and platelet function testing.

treatmentOverview: First-aid management of an acute nosebleed is the most important immediate intervention: sit leaning slightly forward, pinch the soft lower part of the nose firmly for 10-15 uninterrupted minutes, and breathe through the mouth. This compression of Kiesselbach's plexus controls anterior bleeds in 80-90% of cases. Cold compresses on the nape of the neck are not evidence-based. For recurrent anterior epistaxis with a visible vessel at Kiesselbach's plexus, ENT guidelines recommend silver nitrate chemical cautery in clinic under topical anesthesia, which controls recurrence in 65-80% of cases. Only one side of the septum should be cauterized per session to avoid septal perforation. For bleeds not controlled by compression, anterior nasal packing with non-adherent foam or Merocel ribbon gauze packs is the emergency standard, with the pack retained for 24-48 hours. Posterior bleeds refractory to anterior packing require a posterior nasal pack (Foley catheter balloon or commercial bilateral pack) or endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation, which controls posterior bleeding in 90-95% of cases with a lower complication rate than packing alone. For HHT, treatment follows the HHT International guidelines 2020: bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) intranasal spray, laser photocoagulation, and systemic tranexamic acid are the main disease-modifying options. Underlying causes — hypertension, anticoagulation, coagulopathy, nasal inflammation — must be addressed concurrently for any treatment to be durable. NICE 2023 guidance recommends routine review of nasal spray technique in patients on intranasal corticosteroids with epistaxis.

medicalTreatments.name: Silver nitrate chemical cautery
medicalTreatments.description: Application of a 75% silver nitrate stick to the bleeding vessel or prominent vessels at Kiesselbach's plexus after topical lidocaine/xylometazoline spray. The most effective outpatient treatment for anterior recurrent epistaxis. Only one septum wall cauterized per visit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrent epistaxis by 65-80% at 8 weeks; superior to nasal lubrication alone in RCTs (PMID 21447472)

medicalTreatments.name: Tranexamic acid (topical or systemic)
medicalTreatments.description: Antifibrinolytic agent that prevents clot dissolution. Topical tranexamic acid soaked gauze controls acute epistaxis in 70% of cases when applied for 10 minutes. Oral tranexamic acid 500-1,500 mg three times daily is used as maintenance therapy for HHT and coagulopathic epistaxis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Topical tranexamic acid controls acute anterior epistaxis within 10 minutes in 73% of patients in the NOSEBLEED trial (PMID 34362513)

medicalTreatments.name: Nasal saline irrigation and lubricant (petroleum jelly, mupirocin)
medicalTreatments.description: Saline nasal rinse twice daily maintains mucosal moisture; petroleum jelly applied to the anterior septum at bedtime reduces crusting and vessel fragility. Recommended as first-line for mild recurrent childhood epistaxis; also used alongside cautery as maintenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence frequency by 30-50% versus no treatment in observational studies; most evidence in children with idiopathic anterior epistaxis

medicalTreatments.name: Anterior nasal packing (Merocel or bismuth iodoform paraffin paste ribbon gauze)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when external compression fails or when bleeding point is not visible. Merocel foam expands when wet to compress nasal mucosa. Pack retained for 24-48 hours; antibiotic prophylaxis is commonly given. Causes significant discomfort; removal can re-trigger bleeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls anterior bleeding in 70-80% of cases when compression fails; posterior packs required in 10-15% when anterior packing is insufficient

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab intranasal spray (for HHT)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody applied as a nasal spray (1 mg/mL, 0.1 mL per nostril, weekly for 12 weeks) reduces telangiectasia vascularity in HHT. Evidence from the ATERO trial and multiple case series in HHT centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces monthly epistaxis duration by 50% in HHT at 12 weeks in the ATERO trial; effects last 3-6 months before retreatment needed

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic clip or bipolar diathermy ligation of the sphenopalatine artery and its branches through the posterior nasal cavity under general anesthetic. Gold-standard treatment for recurrent posterior epistaxis or failure of nasal packing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls posterior bleeding in 90-95% of cases; significantly lower morbidity than packing; same-day discharge possible; preferred by AAO-HNS over arterial embolization in most cases

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic laser photocoagulation (for HHT)
surgicalOptions.description: Nd:YAG, KTP, or diode laser destroys nasal telangiectasias through a rigid nasal endoscope under local or general anesthetic. Reduces bleeding frequency in HHT but does not cure the disease — new telangiectasias form over 6-18 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces epistaxis frequency by 50-70% at 6 months in HHT; requires repeat treatment every 6-24 months for disease control

surgicalOptions.name: Arterial embolization (interventional radiology)
surgicalOptions.description: Catheter-based selective embolization of the internal maxillary artery is used when endoscopic ligation fails or is not available. Carries 2-3% risk of facial ischemia or stroke from non-target embolization.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls epistaxis in 85-95% initially; re-bleeding rate of 10-20% at 1 year from collateral vessel recruitment

surgicalOptions.name: Septal dermoplasty (Young's procedure or skin grafting for HHT)
surgicalOptions.description: Closure of the anterior nasal aperture (Young's total closure) or replacement of nasal mucosal telangiectasias with skin graft (Saunders procedure) in HHT patients with severe, refractory epistaxis. Near-complete abolition of epistaxis in Young's procedure but patients must breathe through the mouth.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Young's operation reduces epistaxis to near zero in 90% of HHT patients; significant quality-of-life trade-off from nasal closure

alternativeTreatments.name: Humidification of bedroom air
alternativeTreatments.description: Maintaining indoor relative humidity at 45-55% with a cold-mist humidifier during heating season reduces nasal mucosal dryness, the most common trigger of recurrent anterior epistaxis in children and adults. Safe and inexpensive as an adjunct to medical treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: An ENT specialist should evaluate recurrent epistaxis (two or more episodes per month), any episode lasting more than 20 minutes despite first-aid, posterior bleeds running down the throat, unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction, or suspicion of HHT. Children failing first-aid and simple measures for more than 6 months should be referred for cautery assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: An otolaryngologist with experience in nasal endoscopy and epistaxis management handles most cases. For HHT, referral to a dedicated HHT Center of Excellence (recognized by the HHT Foundation International) is strongly recommended for multidisciplinary screening of pulmonary, hepatic, and cerebral AVMs alongside nasal treatment.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency access to ENT for nasal packing in uncontrolled bleeding
Operating theater for endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation under general anesthetic
Interventional radiology for arterial embolization in refractory or recurrent posterior bleeding
Hematology service for management of coagulopathy contributing to epistaxis
keyFacilities: ENT outpatient clinic with silver nitrate cautery capability
Emergency department with ENT on-call for acute packing
HHT Centers of Excellence for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia
Dedicated nose clinic for recurrent cases needing investigation and endoscopy
preventionStrategies: Apply a small amount of petroleum jelly (Vaseline) to the anterior nasal septum at bedtime during dry weather or heating season to maintain mucosal moisture.
Use a bedroom humidifier to maintain indoor humidity at 45-55% during winter months when central heating dries nasal mucosa.
Teach correct nasal spray technique for intranasal corticosteroids — angle the nozzle toward the lateral nasal wall, not the septum, to reduce vessel irritation.
Control blood pressure with antihypertensive medication; uncontrolled hypertension prolongs nosebleeds and increases recurrence frequency.
Review anticoagulant doses and INR levels regularly with prescribing physicians to ensure therapeutic rather than supratherapeutic anticoagulation.
lifestyleModifications: Learn the correct first-aid technique: lean slightly forward, pinch the soft lower nose firmly for 10-15 minutes, breathe through mouth — avoid tilting the head back, which directs blood into the airway.
Keep short fingernails and discourage nose-picking in children — digital trauma is the dominant cause of recurrent childhood anterior epistaxis.
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) unless medically necessary; paracetamol does not impair platelet function and is a safer analgesic for patients with recurrent epistaxis.
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated to reduce nasal venous congestion that can trigger nocturnal bleeds.
Carry topical tranexamic acid gauze or a haemostat dressing for rapid self-management if bleeds occur frequently away from medical care.

recommended: Vitamin K-rich foods (leafy greens, broccoli, Brussels sprouts) for patients on warfarin to maintain a stable dietary vitamin K intake and prevent INR fluctuation
Iron-rich foods (lean meat, legumes, fortified cereals, dark leafy greens) to replenish iron stores if recurrent bleeding has caused iron-deficiency anemia
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, kiwi) to support collagen synthesis in nasal mucosa and capillary wall integrity
avoid: Alcohol, which causes vasodilation and impairs platelet aggregation — even moderate intake worsens bleeding in patients with recurrent epistaxis or coagulopathy
Excessive omega-3 supplements (fish oil above 3 g/day) at doses that can prolong bleeding time in combination with antiplatelet medications
Hot, spicy foods that cause mucosal vasodilation and temporarily increase nosebleed risk in susceptible individuals

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is safe and does not typically increase nosebleed frequency. High-intensity exercise can raise blood pressure transiently and trigger anterior bleeds in susceptible individuals. If exercise-induced bleeds are frequent, blood pressure should be assessed during exercise. Contact sports carry a risk of nasal trauma and are best avoided in patients with uncorrected vessel abnormalities or active disease.
dailyManagement: Apply nasal lubricant (petroleum jelly, naseptin, or nasal saline gel) to the anterior septum each night as preventive mucosal maintenance.
Monitor blood pressure at home if hypertension is a contributing factor; target systolic below 140 mmHg to reduce bleeding severity.
Record each bleeding episode (date, duration, volume, trigger, laterality) in a bleed diary to provide objective data for the ENT surgeon and aid treatment response assessment.
Check INR monthly or per prescriber instructions if on warfarin; contact the anticoagulation team if values exceed 3.0 and epistaxis becomes more frequent.
Follow post-cautery instructions: avoid nose-blowing, hot showers, and strenuous activity for 48 hours after silver nitrate treatment to allow mucosal healing.
prognosis: The prognosis for most recurrent epistaxis is excellent. Children with idiopathic anterior epistaxis have an 85-90% spontaneous resolution rate by puberty without intervention, and silver nitrate cautery achieves control in 65-80% of those requiring treatment. Adults with hypertension-associated epistaxis see substantially reduced recurrence rates with optimal blood pressure control and topical measures. Posterior epistaxis managed by endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation has a 90-95% success rate. HHT epistaxis is the most refractory subtype; it requires ongoing specialist management, but laser photocoagulation, bevacizumab spray, and systemic tranexamic acid maintain acceptable quality of life in 70-80% of patients. Iron-deficiency anemia from chronic loss responds promptly to iron replacement and epistaxis control. The very small proportion of patients in whom epistaxis remains uncontrolled despite full treatment are candidates for Young's nasal closure procedure, which is near-curative for HHT. Untreated epistaxis with significant blood loss causes chronic anemia, fatigue, and rarely cardiovascular compromise.
recoveryTimeline: Acute epistaxis controlled by compression or cautery resolves within 15-20 minutes in most cases. Post-cautery mucosal healing takes 48-72 hours; full vessel obliteration and durability assessed at 4-8 weeks. Anterior nasal packing is removed in 24-48 hours with immediate bleeding relief in most patients. After endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation, patients return home the same day and nosebleed-free rates are assessed at 4-6 weeks.
complications: Iron-deficiency anemia from chronic recurrent blood loss, which is often underrecognized and found incidentally on blood count; serum ferritin is the most sensitive marker.
Aspiration of blood during a severe posterior epistaxis, causing airway compromise and aspiration pneumonitis, particularly in elderly patients with impaired swallowing.

Septal perforation as a complication of bilateral simultaneous cautery, aggressive packing, or nasal surgical trauma — causes persistent crusting, whistling, and recurrent anterior bleeding at the perforation edge.
Sinusitis and toxic shock syndrome as rare complications of prolonged nasal packing retained over 48-72 hours, requiring antibiotic prophylaxis coverage with staphylococcal cover.
Hemodynamic shock from massive posterior epistaxis, particularly in anticoagulated elderly patients — requires emergency resuscitation and packed red cell transfusion.

supportResources.name: HHT Foundation International
supportResources.description: The primary patient advocacy organization for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia; provides a directory of HHT Centers of Excellence and patient education resources.

supportResources.name: ENT UK — Nosebleed (Epistaxis) Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient leaflet on first aid, causes, and treatment of nosebleeds from the UK ENT professional association.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Nosebleeds
supportResources.description: Patient guide on nosebleed self-management, warning signs, and ENT specialist referral criteria from the US ENT professional society.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A deviated nasal septum causes turbulent airflow and drying at the deviation apex, predisposing to epistaxis, but the septum itself is not the bleeding source — the vessels at Kiesselbach's plexus are. Septal deviation is a contributing risk factor; septoplasty improves nasal airflow and may reduce vessel trauma but is not the primary treatment for epistaxis unless the deviation is severe and directly exposing vessels.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasal Polyps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nasal polyps cause unilateral or bilateral nasal obstruction and rhinorrhea; large polyps may bleed, but recurrent epistaxis is not a primary feature. Unilateral nasal bleeding with obstruction in an adult is more concerning for a nasal tumor or angiofibroma than a polyp. Endoscopy and CT distinguish the two conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypertension is strongly associated with severe epistaxis but does not directly cause the bleeding — the vessel rupture occurs at Kiesselbach's plexus or the sphenopalatine territory regardless of the pressure. High blood pressure prolongs bleeding by impairing vasoconstriction. Blood pressure should be controlled alongside local nasal treatment; treating hypertension alone does not cure the underlying vessel fragility.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis causes persistent nasal congestion, mucopurulent discharge, and facial pain; it predisposes to epistaxis through mucosal inflammation, but the inflammatory discharge is distinct from frank epistaxis. If bleeding is the presenting symptom, it should prompt examination for a separate bleeding source rather than attributing it solely to sinusitis.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal Polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: Nasal polyps cause mucosal inflammation and congestion that predisposes to epistaxis; endoscopic management of polyps reduces nasal bleeding in affected patients.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared nasal inflammatory pathway; sinusitis-driven mucosal engorgement and crusting increases epistaxis frequency in susceptible individuals.

relatedConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
relatedConditions.relevance: Anatomical nasal septal deviation concentrates airflow turbulence and drying, exposing vessels at Kiesselbach's plexus to mechanical stress and increased epistaxis risk.

relatedConditions.name: Sleep Apnea
relatedConditions.relevance: CPAP therapy for sleep apnea frequently causes nasal dryness and mucosal irritation, leading to recurrent epistaxis; humidified CPAP and nasal lubricants manage this complication.

faqs.question: Why do I keep getting nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: Recurrent nosebleeds most commonly arise from dry nasal mucosa, prominent blood vessels on the nasal septum, or nose-picking. In adults over 50, uncontrolled high blood pressure and blood-thinning medications are frequent causes. A structural cause (polyp or tumor) or inherited bleeding disorder should be investigated if bleeding is frequent, prolonged, or does not respond to first-aid.

faqs.question: What is the correct first aid for a nosebleed?
faqs.answer: Sit leaning slightly forward, pinch the soft lower part of the nose firmly (not the bony bridge), and breathe through the mouth for 10-15 uninterrupted minutes. Do not tilt the head back; this directs blood into the airway. Avoid checking if bleeding has stopped until the full time has elapsed. If bleeding continues beyond 20 minutes, seek medical attention.

faqs.question: What causes recurrent nosebleeds in adults?
faqs.answer: In adults, the most common causes are uncontrolled hypertension, anticoagulant medications (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban), intranasal steroid sprays aimed at the septum, nasal mucosal dryness, and hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT). An ENT endoscopy identifies prominent vessels, perforations, or mass lesions that explain recurrence in 20-30% of cases.

faqs.question: What causes recurrent nosebleeds in children?
faqs.answer: Most childhood nosebleeds arise from dry nasal mucosa and nose-picking at the front of the nasal septum (Little's area). Low indoor humidity from central heating is a major seasonal trigger. Von Willebrand disease is found in 5-10% of children investigated for very frequent bleeds. Most children improve spontaneously with puberty without intervention.

faqs.question: How do ENT doctors treat recurrent nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: The most effective outpatient treatment is silver nitrate chemical cautery — a pointed stick applied to the visible bleeding vessel under local anesthetic nasal spray. This controls recurrence in 65-80% of cases. Only one side of the nasal septum is treated at each visit. Additional treatments include topical nasal lubricants, blood pressure control, and in severe cases, endoscopic surgery.

faqs.question: What is hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT)?
faqs.answer: HHT is an inherited genetic condition (autosomal dominant) causing fragile blood vessels called telangiectasias in the nasal lining, skin, lungs, liver, and brain. It affects 1 in 5,000-8,000 people and causes recurrent nosebleeds in over 95% of affected individuals, often many times per week. Diagnosis is clinical; dedicated HHT centers provide multidisciplinary screening and treatment.

faqs.question: Can high blood pressure cause nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: High blood pressure is strongly associated with more severe and prolonged nosebleeds but does not directly rupture nasal vessels. It impairs the body's ability to constrict bleeding vessels and is found in 60-70% of adults presenting to emergency departments with epistaxis. Blood pressure control reduces bleeding severity but does not substitute for local nasal treatment.

faqs.question: Should I go to the emergency department for a nosebleed?
faqs.answer: Go to the emergency department if: bleeding does not stop after 20 minutes of correct first-aid compression, blood is going down the back of the throat, you feel faint or are losing large amounts of blood, you are on blood thinners and bleeding heavily, or you have had a head injury. Most nosebleeds stop with home first-aid within 10-15 minutes.

faqs.question: Will blood thinners make my nosebleeds worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) and antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) all prolong the time a nosebleed takes to stop. Patients on these medications have 3-5 times more epistaxis episodes than those not on anticoagulation. Dose review with the prescribing doctor may reduce recurrence, but stopping anticoagulation without medical advice is dangerous.

faqs.question: What is the sphenopalatine artery and why does it cause nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: The sphenopalatine artery is the main blood supply to the back of the nasal cavity. Posterior nosebleeds arise from this artery and run down the throat rather than out of the front of the nose. They are more serious than front-of-nose bleeds, harder to control with simple compression, and often require endoscopic surgery to clip or cauterize the artery under general anesthetic.

faqs.question: Can nasal sprays cause nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intranasal corticosteroid sprays cause mucosal thinning and vessel irritation when aimed at the nasal septum rather than the lateral wall. Decongestant sprays (oxymetazoline) cause rebound congestion and vascular stress. Spraying toward the outside of the nose rather than the septum, and limiting decongestant use to 3 days, prevents most spray-related nosebleeds.

faqs.question: How do I prevent nosebleeds in dry weather?
faqs.answer: Apply a small amount of petroleum jelly (Vaseline) to the front of the nasal septum each night using a clean fingertip or cotton bud to maintain mucosal moisture. Use a bedroom humidifier to keep humidity above 45%. Regular isotonic saline nasal rinses twice daily reduce crusting and maintain nasal lining health throughout dry seasons.

faqs.question: Can recurrent nosebleeds cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Frequent or heavy recurrent nosebleeds cause iron-deficiency anemia through cumulative blood loss, which may not be recognized if the blood is swallowed rather than visible. Symptoms are fatigue, pallor, reduced exercise tolerance, and breathlessness. A full blood count and serum ferritin confirm the diagnosis; iron supplementation and nosebleed treatment are given together.

faqs.question: Is nasal cautery painful?
faqs.answer: Silver nitrate cautery for nosebleeds is performed in the ENT clinic after a topical anesthetic and decongestant nasal spray. Most patients describe mild discomfort or pressure but not significant pain during the 5-minute procedure. A burning sensation is felt during application. Post-cautery, the area is sore for 24-48 hours; over-the-counter paracetamol provides adequate pain relief.

faqs.question: Do children grow out of nosebleeds?
faqs.answer: Most children with anterior idiopathic nosebleeds do improve substantially around puberty, when nasal mucosal maturation reduces fragility. Studies show 85-90% of childhood epistaxis resolves without specific treatment by adolescence. A minority require silver nitrate cautery if bleeds are very frequent, prolonged, or causing anemia before that natural improvement occurs.

metaDescription: Recurrent nosebleeds affect 60% of people. Anterior vs posterior causes, first-aid, silver nitrate cautery, HHT diagnosis, and ENT referral criteria reviewed.
